coredata.dc:title: Enhanced neuronal activity in mouse motor cortex with microbubbles’ oscillations by transcranial focused ultrasound stimulation
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Zhiwei
Li, Dapeng
Feng, Yang
Xu, Tianqi
Wu, Shan
Li, Yibao
Bouakaz, Ayache
Wan, Mingxi
Zhang, Siyuan
coredata.dc:description: Microbubbles (MBs) are known to serve as an amplifier of the mechanical effects of ultrasound, which combined with ultrasound are widely used in brain. The goal of this study is to investigate the effect of oscillating MBs on the neuronal activity in the central nervous system (CNS) of mammals. The motor cortex of mice brain was subjected to ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs, and evoked electromyogram signals were recorded. A c-fos immunofluorescence assay was performed to evaluate the neuronal activation in the region of ultrasound stimulation. BBB integrity during ultrasound stimulation with MBs was assessed in this study. Moreover, the safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs was examined. Using ultrasound at 620 kHz, the injection of MBs significantly increased the success rate of motor response from 0.065 ± 0.06 to 0.28 ± 0.10 when stimulation was applied at 0.12 MPa and from 0.38 ± 0.09 to 0.77 ± 0.18 at 0.25 MPa (p < 0.001). The results of the c-fos immunofluorescence assay showed that the mean densities of c-fos+ cells were significantly increased from 15.67 ± 3.51 to 53.01 ± 9.54 at 0.12 MPa acoustic pressure. At 0.25 MPa, the mean density of c-fos + cells was 81 ± 10.97 without MBs and it significantly increased to 124.12 ± 25.71 with MBs (p < 0.05). Enhanced neuronal activities were observed with 0.12 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs, while the integrity of BBB was not compromised, but 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs resulted in BBB disruption. These findings reveal that the oscillations of MBs can enhance neuronal activity in the CNS of mammals, and may provide an insight into the application of MBs combined with ultrasound in brain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound stimulation
Microbubbles
Motor response
c-fos
Neuronal activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Success rate of motor response after ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs
Motor response properties evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs
Comparison of c-fos activity in US stimulated brain sections with and without MBs
Integrity of BBB after ultrasound stimulation with MBs
Safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the experimental setup. (a) Block diagram of ultrasound stimulation and EMG acquisition. Measured acoustic intensity field profile of the ultrasound transducer (lower left). (b) Ultrasound sequence used for the stimulation.

Motor response evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs. (a) Typical EMG signals with and without MBs. (b) Mean success rates with and without MBs as a function of acoustic pressure (n = 10 mice per group). Group differences were analyzed by two-way repeated ANOVA, with * for p < 0.05, ** for p < 0.01, *** for p < 0.001, and N.S. for not significant.

Motor response properties evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs. Histograms illustrating EMG response latency, EMG response latency, and peak EMG amplitude in response to ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs (n = 10 mice per group). Group differences were analyzed by two-way repeated ANOVA, with * for p < 0.05, ** for p < 0.01, *** for p < 0.001, and N.S. for not significant.

Neuronal activity evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs. (a) Diagram illustrating the anatomical site of ultrasound stimulation and the location of the brain sections for performing immunofluorescence assay (IFA) using antibodies against c-fos. (b) Brain section obtained from the location of ultrasound stimulation. The ellipse indicates the region of ultrasound stimulation, and the rectangle indicates the region of the obtained fluorescent images of c-fos+ cells. (c) Representative fluorescent images of c-fos+ cells (red) obtained from the region of ultrasound stimulation. Cell nuclei were stained by DAPI (blue). (d) The mean density of c-fos+ cells in response to ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs (n = 3 mice per group). Group difference was analyzed by two-way repeated ANOVA, with * for p < 0.05, ** for p < 0.01, *** for p < 0.001, and N.S. for not significant. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

BBB opening evaluation for the ultrasound stimulation with MBs. (a) Representative EB images of the horizontal (top) and the coronal plane (bottom) indicating BBB integrity. (b) Histogram of the EB extravasation for BBB opening quantification (n = 3 mice per group). Group differences were analyzed by one-way repeated ANOVA, with * for p < 0.05, ** for p < 0.01, *** for p < 0.001, and N.S. for not significant.

The safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs. HE staining of the brain sections under (a) 0.12 MPa and (b) 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs. TUNEL staining (DAPI is in blue, apoptotic cells are in green) for the brain sections under (c) 0.12 MPa and (d) 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Enhanced neuronal activity in mouse motor cortex with microbubbles’ oscillations by transcranial focused ultrasound stimulation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UMR 1253, iBrain, Université de Tours, Inserm, France
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UMR 1253, iBrain, Université de Tours, Inserm, France

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microbubbles (MBs) are known to serve as an amplifier of the mechanical effects of ultrasound, which combined with ultrasound are widely used in brain. The goal of this study is to investigate the effect of oscillating MBs on the neuronal activity in the central nervous system (CNS) of mammals. The motor cortex of mice brain was subjected to ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs, and evoked electromyogram signals were recorded. A c-fos immunofluorescence assay was performed to evaluate the neuronal activation in the region of ultrasound stimulation. BBB integrity during ultrasound stimulation with MBs was assessed in this study. Moreover, the safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs was examined. Using ultrasound at 620 kHz, the injection of MBs significantly increased the success rate of motor response from 0.065 ± 0.06 to 0.28 ± 0.10 when stimulation was applied at 0.12 MPa and from 0.38 ± 0.09 to 0.77 ± 0.18 at 0.25 MPa (p < 0.001). The results of the c-fos immunofluorescence assay showed that the mean densities of c-fos+ cells were significantly increased from 15.67 ± 3.51 to 53.01 ± 9.54 at 0.12 MPa acoustic pressure. At 0.25 MPa, the mean density of c-fos + cells was 81 ± 10.97 without MBs and it significantly increased to 124.12 ± 25.71 with MBs (p < 0.05). Enhanced neuronal activities were observed with 0.12 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs, while the integrity of BBB was not compromised, but 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs resulted in BBB disruption. These findings reveal that the oscillations of MBs can enhance neuronal activity in the CNS of mammals, and may provide an insight into the application of MBs combined with ultrasound in brain.

ce:section.ce:para: Focused ultrasound (FUS) was proved to be an effective approach for the treatment of neurological disease decades ago [1,2]. So far, the application of therapeutic ultrasound in brain has been extensively investigated [3,4]. A large part of these applications, such as opening of the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and sonothrombolysis, are based on the mechanical effects of ultrasound [5–7]. Gaseous microbubbles (MBs) are routinely used as a contrast agent for ultrasonic diagnostic purposes, which can oscillate under ultrasonic excitation, generating additional mechanical effects. Due to the fact that few cavitation nuclei exist in blood and soft tissues, MBs are always introduced in brain as cavitation nuclei to achieve sufficient biological effects at low acoustic pressures [7–9]. Although no tissue and/or cell-level damage have been observed when low intensity ultrasound and MBs are used alone or in combination [9,10], it is also necessary to clear their influence on the neuronal activity and neurological function.

In this decade, since Tufail et al. [11] demonstrated that mouse motor cortex could be excited in vivo using ultrasound stimulation, ultrasound has been reported to be an effective tool to modulate neuronal activity in motor cortex [11–13], thalamic area [14,15], vagus nerve [16], and retina [17,18], and it has been deemed to have enormous potential in altering neural activity due to its noninvasive nature, superior spatial resolution and better penetration depth [3,19,20]. Besides preclinical evaluation, human studies have been reported and Legon et al. showed that ultrasound targeting on human primary somatosensory cortex could enhance the somatosensory discrimination abilities and alter electroencephalographic activity [21,22]. Lee et al. [23,24] showed that the sonication of human primary somatosensory cortex could elicit somatosensory sensations and ultrasound stimulation not only excited the sonicated brain area, but also the network of regions involved in visual and higher-order cognitive processes. In addition, ultrasonic neuromodulation is also expected to be an effective, noninvasive, and reversible treatment for psychiatric and neurological diseases, including epilepsy [25,26], essential tremor [27], and major depression [28,29].

Despite abundant research on the application of ultrasonic neuromodulation, the mechanisms involved in ultrasonic neuromodulation remain largely unrevealed. In recent years, numerous studies have indicated that non-thermal effect plays an important role in altering neural activity. It has been confirmed that mechanosensitive ion channels in the xenopus oocyte system [30] and C. elegans [31–33] can be manipulated by ultrasound. More convincingly, when the thermosensitive ion channels were knocked out, the C. elegans still showed behavioral response to ultrasound, whereas knocking out the mechanosensitive ion channels suppressed this behavior [31–33], which further indicated that the main factor to effect neuronal activation is the mechanical effect of ultrasound. Additionally, temperature rise induced by ultrasound stimulation has been reported to be usually less than 0.3 °C [11,34]. Therefore, it is generally accepted that non-thermal mechanisms, especially the mechanical effect of ultrasound, are supposed to be the main potential mechanism of ultrasonic neuromodulation [19,20,33].

In addition to ultrasound, several studies also have shown that the oscillation of MBs might be able to alter neuronal activities. In the study of “sonogenetics” in C. elegans [32], gas-filled MBs were used to amplify ultrasound mechanical deformations. The neurons and processes of worms do not contact the external cuticle, and the MBs were added to the growth media. The authors indicated that the mechanical deformations produced by oscillating MBs likely travel through the cuticle of worms to stimulate neurons. In contrast, MBs were generally introduced by intravenous injection for mammals. Both BBB and astrocytes might be the obstacle between MBs and neurons, and the vessel also have some impact on the oscillation of MBs [35,36]. Thus, it is not a strong proof to illustrate the effect of MBs on neuronal activity in mammals. The studies focused on the influence of BBB opening on the neuronal functions indicated that BBB opening, induced by focused ultrasound combined with microbubbles, can alter the neuron response [37] and visual-motor decision-making behavior [38,39], which might be the evidences that MB oscillation influences the neuronal activity in mammals. However, these neuromodulatory effects are accompanied by BBB opening. Ions, nutrients, and hormones in blood are also able to disrupt neuronal activity [3,40]. Therefore, it is unclear whether the diffusion of blood constituents into brain tissue or the sustained impacts induced by MB oscillation alter the neuronal functions.

Motor response evoked by the stimulation of mouse motor cortex is a traditional measurement to immediately evaluate the effect of stimulation on neuronal function. In the current study, this immediate method was performed to investigate effect of MB oscillation on the neuronal activity. Considering that mechanical effects are the main potential mechanism for ultrasonic neuromodulation, we sought to test whether the introduction of MBs might enhance neuronal activity. Therefore, referring to our preliminary experiment, the lower acoustic pressures (0.12 MPa and 0.25 MPa), which show lower success rates of motor response to ultrasound stimulation, was used in this work. Besides, using the lower acoustic pressure (0.12 MPa) can also exclude the influence of BBB opening.

The purpose of this study was to investigate whether the presence of MBs can enhance the neuronal activity in the central nervous system (CNS) of mammals. The motor cortex of mice brain was subjected to ultrasound stimulation, and motor responses with and without MBs were compared. To further confirm the effect of MBs on the neuronal activity, evoked neuronal activity was also examined by antibodies against c-fos. During ultrasound stimulation with MBs, BBB integrity was assessed, and additionally, in order to evaluate its safety, hematoxylin-eosin (HE) and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase-mediated dUTP-biotin nick end labeling assay (TUNEL) staining were performed.

All procedures described in this study were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of School of Life Science and Technology of Xi’an Jiaotong University. Adult BALB/c mice were anesthetized in an induction chamber with isoflurane (2%, 0.5 L/min O2), and then were housed in a mouse stereotaxic apparatus, where the anesthesia was continued through a custom anesthesia mask. The hair on the mice head was shaved by scissors. The targeted brain region for ultrasound stimulation was the motor cortex of the mouse (bregma, 1 mm lateral to midline). Typically, five minutes before ultrasound stimulation, the isoflurane level was reduced to 0.5%, resulting in a light anesthesia level.

The schematic of focused ultrasound (FUS) experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 (a), and the stimulation pulsing sequence in Fig. 1(b) which consisted of ultrasound pulses of 2 ms duration (PD) with a 250 Hz pulse repetition frequency (PRF), and total sonication duration (SD) of 400 ms. The pulses were generated by a two-channel function generator (DG5072, Rigol Inc., Beijing, China) and amplified by an RF power amplifier (25A250A, AR Europe, Bothell, USA) before being sent to a custom 620 kHz frequency FUS transducer (100 mm in aperture, 80 mm in focal depth). The acoustic intensity field of the FUS transducer was measured separately using a needle hydrophone (HNR-1000) in a degassed water. Two different acoustic pressures were used in the study: 0.12 MPa and 0.25 MPa. These values correspond to peak rarefactional pressures at the focus of the transducer after mouse skull attenuation. Equivalent spatial-peak pulse-average intensities (ISPPA) are 0.48 W/cm2 and 2.11 W/cm2.

Tungsten electrodes were inserted into the triceps muscles of mouse forelimbs to record electromyogram (EMG) signals evoked by ultrasound stimulation. EMG signals were amplified with a gain of 1000 and bandpass filtered between 300 Hz and 5 kHz using an amplifier (model 1700, A-M Systems, Inc., Sequim, WA, USA), and acquired at a 10 kHz sampling frequency using a data acquisition card (1550A, Axon Instruments, San Jose, CA, USA). MATLAB (MathWorks, Natick, MA, USA) was used to process the EMG signals. Twenty ultrasound stimulations as described above in 8 s intervals were performed for each condition. For the group of ultrasound stimulation with MBs, 60 μL MBs (2 × 108 bubbles/ml and mean size was 2.5 μm, Bracco SpA, Milan, Italy) were administered intravenously before applying the ultrasound stimulation. The peak EMG amplitude was defined as the maximum value within each motor response. The time period in which the EMG signal was three times higher than standard deviations of background noise was defined as the EMG response duration. The EMG response latency was defined as the time between the onset of stimulation and the beginning of contraction. Success rate was defined as the percentage of the number of successful stimuli of the total number of stimuli. When the peak EMG amplitude was higher than three standard deviations above the background noise, it was regarded as a successful stimulation.

Evans blue (EB) quantification [41] was performed to assess the influence of ultrasound stimulation with MBs on BBB integrity. Prior to ultrasound stimulation with MBs, EB dye (1% in saline) was intravenously administered (3 mL/kg). Using the same ultrasound stimulation parameters as described above, twenty ultrasound stimulations in 8 s intervals were applied. One hour later, the animals were sacrificed and perfused with saline. Mouse brains were removed and stored in formamide (100 mg/1 mL) overnight at 60 °C in order to extract EB from brain tissue. Then the samples were centrifuged for 20 min at 14,000 rpm, and the EB concentration was determined spectrophotometrically at 620 nm.

An immunofluorescence assay (IFA) with antibodies against c-fos was performed to evaluate the neuronal activity induced by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs. In order to investigate the safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs, HE and TUNEL staining were performed (n = 3 per group).

The same ultrasound stimulation parameters as described above were applied and the motor cortex of mice was stimulated for 30 min in 8 s intervals. For the stimulation with MBs group, the MBs were administered by intravenous injection (2 μL/g) every 10 min to ensure the presence of MBs during total stimulation. For HE staining, the mice were sacrificed immediately after stimulation, while for IFA and TUNEL staining the mice were sacrificed after 30 min and 24 h after stimulation, respectively. Following perfusion with 4% paraformaldehyde, mouse brains were removed and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde at 4 °C for 24 h. For HE staining, brain sections of 5 μm in thickness were cut using a vibratome, while for IFA and TUNEL staining brain sections were cut at 40 μm in thickness using a cryotome. For IFA staining, brain sections were incubated with the primary antibody against c-fos (1:200, ab214672, Abcam) at 4 °C for 12 h, and then were washed and incubated with the secondary antibody (1:1000, A32794, Invitrogen) for 1 h at room temperature. Standard HE was performed, and TUNEL staining was performed with a cell death detection kit (#11684795910, Roche). Histological images were acquired using a light microscope or fluorescence microscope, and analyzed with ImageJ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Success rate of motor response after ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs
ce:section.ce:para: EMG signals in response to the ultrasound stimulation of the motor cortex with and without MBs were acquired. Fig. 2 (a) shows typical EMG signals from the triceps muscles of mouse forelimbs evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs at the acoustic pressure of 0.25 MPa, while the synchronous signals of ultrasound stimulation indicating the onset time of stimuli are shown at the bottom. The results revealed a significant effect of MBs and acoustic pressure on the success rate of motor response. It was found that MB oscillation produced higher success rate (p < 0.001; Fig. 2(b)), and that the success rate was generally proportional to the sonication intensity (p < 0.001; Fig. 2(b)), which is consistent with previous studies [11,42]. The success rate of motor response to the 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation was 0.38 ± 0.09, whereas it significantly increased to 0.77 ± 0.18 when MBs were introduced in the sonication region. In addition, the presence of MBs significantly increased the success rate of ultrasound stimulation from 0.065 ± 0.06 to 0.28 ± 0.10 when the acoustic pressure was 0.12 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Motor response properties evoked by ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the success rate, several motor response properties were characterized by calculating the EMG response latency, EMG response duration, and peak EMG amplitude. As it can be seen in Fig. 3 (a), regardless of the presence of MBs within the region of stimulation, the EMG response latency significantly decreased when the acoustic pressure was increased from 0.12 MPa to 0.25 MPa (p < 0.001), but no significant difference was found in the response latency between ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs (p > 0.05). The results plotted in Fig. 3(b, c) show that both stimulation acoustic amplitude and MBs presence did not influence the EMG response duration and peak EMG amplitude (p > 0.05). At an acoustic pressure of 0.25 MPa, an upward trend of the EMG response duration and peak EMG amplitude of motor response appeared after introducing MBs in the region of stimulation, but the differences with the other groups were not statistically significant (p > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of c-fos activity in US stimulated brain sections with and without MBs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further illustrate the effect of MBs’ oscillations on neuronal activity, c-fos expression as a marker of neural activation was analyzed using the c-fos immunofluorescence assay in the region of ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs. Representative fluorescent images of the c-fos immunofluorescence assay and the mean density of c-fos+ cells in response to 0.12 MPa and 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with and without MBs can be seen in Fig. 4 (c, d). Numerous c-fos+ cells were detected after 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs, while the c-fos expression was significantly lower for the 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation alone group (control mean c-fos+ cell density = 81 ± 10.97 cells/0.58 mm2 versus US stim with MBs = 124.12 ± 25.71 cells/0.58 mm2; p < 0.05). Using 0.12 MPa ultrasound stimulation, MBs’ oscillations significantly increased the density of c-fos+ cells in the region of stimulation compared to the US stimulation in absence of MBs (control mean c-fos+ cell density = 15.67 ± 3.51 cells/0.58 mm2 versus US stim with MBs = 53.01 ± 9.54 cells/0.58 mm2; p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integrity of BBB after ultrasound stimulation with MBs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fact that ultrasound and MBs are currently investigated as a tool to alter the permeability of the BBB, BBB integrity in ultrasound stimulation with MBs was assessed. The quantification of EB leakage showed that the MB-associated ultrasound stimulation at 0.12 MPa acoustic pressure did not cause disruption of the BBB as no leakage was observed. However, the EB extravasation was significantly higher in the group of 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs than that in the control group and group of 0.12 MPa ultrasound stimulation (P < 0.001; Fig. 5 (b)), indicating that 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs would disrupt BBB integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety of ultrasound stimulation with MBs
ce:section.ce:para: The histological analysis based on HE and TUNEL staining is presented in Fig. 6 . Following 0.25 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs for 30 min, no tissue damage, hemorrhage, and apoptosis in the region of stimulation was observed, indicating that ultrasound stimulation with MBs did not result in morphological and/or cellular-level damages.

In the present study, it was demonstrated that the oscillation of MBs was capable of enhancing the neuronal activity in mammals CNS. The key point of the present research is that the introduction of MBs can effectively promote the success rate of motor response to ultrasound stimulation and c-fos activity, which indicated that the existence of MBs would induce increased neuronal activity. There have been various studies indicating that the mechanical effect of ultrasound is supposed to be the main potential mechanism of ultrasonic neuromodulation [19,20,33]. The enhanced neuronal activity induced by MBs might also be related to the additional mechanical effects induced by the oscillation of MBs. The previous studies [43–45] indicated that MBs pulsating in an ultrasound field radiate acoustic pressure into the surrounding medium, which may generate, in addition to the impinging acoustic pressure from the ultrasound transducer, additional mechanical action to the proximal neurons. In general, the average distance between neuron and capillary in mammalian cortex is 13–20 μm [46–49]. Simulations carried out by Krasovitskia et al. indicated that the scattered pressure from the pulsating bubble could even be several times greater than the ultrasonic pressure amplitude [43]. Therefore, the scattered pressure from the pulsating bubbles was supposed to be a potential reason for the enhanced neuronal activity induced by MBs. In addition to the scattered acoustic pressure from MBs, acoustic microstreaming and shear stress are other mechanical effects that are produced by MBs, but these effects can only influence cells extremely close to or in contact with the MBs [8,9,50,51]. Thus, these effects might not play a significant role in this study, as the injected MBs remain in the systemic circulation and do not extravasate to reach the neurons [46–49]. Bubble-vessel wall interactions during ultrasound exposure can also produce vessel deformations, such as distention and invagination [52–56], which might also be another potential mechanical action. According to the work of Ibsen et al. [32], mechanical deformations caused by the ultrasound–microbubble interaction could travel at least 25.5 μm in the worm, thereby activating neurons. Similarly, the average distance of 13–20 μm [46–49] between neuron and capillary possibly does not obstruct the propagation of mechanical deformations of vessel to neurons.

Disruption of the BBB will help ions, nutrients, and hormones in blood to diffuse into the brain tissue, which may also disrupt neuronal activity [3,40]. BBB integrity during ultrasound stimulation with MBs was assessed in this study. According to the results, when acoustic pressure increased from 0.12 MPa to 0.25 MPa, ultrasound stimulation with MBs resulted in BBB disruption. However, enhanced neuronal activity were also observed with 0.12 MPa ultrasound stimulation with MBs, while the integrity of BBB was not compromised at this acoustic pressure, suggesting that the enhanced neuronal activity induced by MBs is likely to be independent of BBB opening. BBB opening is bound up with the ultrasound parameters and the concentration and characteristics of MBs. Consequently, future work should focus on MB-enhanced neuronal activity without BBB opening, using appropriate stimulation parameters and MB concentrations and characteristics.

In this study, the peak EMG amplitude, EMG response latency, and EMG response duration were calculated, and the results showed no variations are induced by the introduction of MBs. According to previous studies, these properties may be affected by stimulus waveforms, intensity, and location [11,34], although there have been some conflicting results. Tufail et al. [11] demonstrated that the peak EMG amplitude was inversely proportional to the ultrasound intensity, and that the EMG response latencies were not affected by either frequency nor intensity, while Li et al. [34] indicated that higher ultrasound intensity would increase the peak EMG amplitude and lower frequency ultrasound stimulation would produce longer EMG response latency. The results of the present study also showed that higher acoustic pressure resulted in shorter EMG response latency. In fact, EMG signals can be affected by a number of factors such as electrode placement, wear and tear of electrodes, and the level of signal noise, which may also influence the comparison of EMG signals across animals and between experimental studies. A thorough investigation on the mechanisms of ultrasound on neuronal activity would immensely help to understand the relationship between motor response properties and stimulus conditions.

Compared to other noninvasive neuromodulation methods, ultrasonic neuromodulation provides superior spatial specificity. However, its resolution is still inferior to several invasive neuromodulation technologies such as deep brain stimulation and optogenetics. Existing methods to improve the resolution of ultrasonic neuromodulation include increasing the ultrasound frequency, enlarging the geometric size of transducer, and sonogenetics. All of these approaches are associated with some limitations, such as increased acoustic attenuation, non-portable, larger acoustic window, and require cell-level genetic alterations. This study demonstrated that the introduction of MBs can produce enhanced neuronal activity even at low acoustic pressures, suggesting that at a lower acoustic intensity, enhanced neuronal activity may be elicited only in the region with the presence of MBs. In other words, the resolution of ultrasonic neuromodulation can be determined not only by the acoustic field of ultrasound transducer, but also by the distribution of MBs. Until today, it has been found that MBs can be manipulated by acoustic waves (e.g., standing waves and acoustic radiation forces), magnetic fields [57–60], and binding MBs to targeting cell receptors or markers by modifying the composition and properties of the MB shell [61,62]. Therefore, a new high-specificity method for ultrasonic neuromodulation might be achieved by concentrating MBs within smaller region or targeting MBs to specific regions.

This study supports the hypothesis that the oscillation of MBs can enhance neuronal activity in the CNS of mammals. MB oscillations induced significantly increased success rates of motor response, and the c-fos immunofluorescence assay showed that the introduction of MBs led to significantly elevated neural activation levels. Moreover, the evaluation of BBB opening suggested that the enhanced neuronal activity induced by MBs is likely to be independent of BBB opening. These results advance the understanding of the effects of MB oscillation on neuronal activity, opening new avenues for the application of MBs in brain.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11874297 and 81827801).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-intensity focused ultrasound surgery of the brain: part 1-a historical perspective with modern applications
Neurosurgery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Jagannathan, N.T. Sanghvi, L.A. Crum, C.P. Yen, R. Medel, A.S. Dumont, J.P. Sheehan, L. Steiner, F. Jolesz, N.F. Kassell, High-intensity focused ultrasound surgery of the brain: part 1-a historical perspective with modern applications, Neurosurgery 64 (2009) 201-210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasonic method of modifying brain structures
Confin. Neurol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Fry, R. Meyers, Ultrasonic Method of Modifying Brain Structures, Confin. Neurol. 22 (1962) 315-327.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound treatment of neurological diseases–current and emerging applications
Nat. Rev. Neurol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Leinenga, C. Langton, R. Nisbet, J. Gotz, Ultrasound treatment of neurological diseases--current and emerging applications, Nat. Rev. Neurol. 12 (2016) 161-174.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of the current therapies, challenges, and future directions of transcranial focused ultrasound technology: advances in diagnosis and treatment
JAMA Neurol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Krishna, F. Sammartino, A. Rezai, A Review of the Current Therapies, Challenges, and Future Directions of Transcranial Focused Ultrasound Technology: Advances in Diagnosis and Treatment, JAMA Neurol (2017).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combining microbubbles and ultrasound for drug delivery to brain tumors: current progress and overview
Theranostics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.L. Liu, C.H. Fan, C.Y. Ting, C.K. Yeh, Combining microbubbles and ultrasound for drug delivery to brain tumors: current progress and overview, Theranostics 4 (2014) 432-444.
